agent1_role: Marina Flores, Event Partnership Manager at Gourmet Gala Foods
agent2_role: Daniel Reed, Operations Director at Fresh Start Catering
generic:
Gourmet Gala Foods is a premium supplier of artisanal food products for both retailers and event organizers. Fresh Start Catering, a mid-sized catering company, has seen its client base nearly double over the past year due to an increased demand for hybrid in-person/virtual corporate events and upscale weddings. This year, Fresh Start Catering needs to renew and restructure its supply contract with Gourmet Gala Foods. Gourmet Gala Foods dominates the city’s market for high-end specialty cheeses and cured meats, but in recent years its delicacies division, which offers curated “gourmet experience kits,” has grown steadily in reputation.  

This will be Fresh Start Catering’s second three-year supply arrangement with Gourmet Gala Foods. The catering company expects around 20 percent growth in event bookings as it broadens its services to online and hybrid event experiences where “event kits” shipped to remote participants are bundled with live event catering. Representing Fresh Start Catering in this year’s negotiations is technical and logistical specialist Daniel Reed, while the dedicated event partnership manager assigned to the account, Marina Flores, represents Gourmet Gala Foods. A central issue in this year’s contract renewal is the balance between traditional bulk ingredients and the newer gourmet experience kits. While Fresh Start Catering still depends heavily on bulk items like cheeses, meats, breads, and condiments for onsite events, the company anticipates a growing demand for the curated kits as more clients request hybrid wedding receptions, corporate meetings, and private celebrations. Each gourmet kit, which includes specialty cheeses, charcuterie selections, seasonal spreads, and promotional packaging, costs $175 more per unit than an equivalent order of bulk ingredients. Bulk ingredients include the basics required for serving up to 30 guests at an in-person event. Kits, by contrast, include high-quality packaging and items portioned for individuals, making them more suitable for shipping and at-home dining experiences. Because Gourmet Gala Foods’ pricing structure is standardized, these costs are non-negotiable.  

In recent years, Gourmet Gala Foods responded to competitors’ innovations by expanding beyond bulk goods into the growing luxury experience-kit market. This pivot required significant investment in packaging infrastructure, supply-chain partners, and customer service capacity. As a result, the company can no longer rely exclusively on bulk orders to sustain profitability. As Gourmet Gala Foods has grown its kit operations, its leadership has imposed new guidelines on how many bulk-only orders can be included in any client’s agreement. Kits generate substantially more profit margin than traditional bulk ingredients, and Marina Flores has been tasked with encouraging clients to shift toward using more kits alongside their regular bulk purchases. While Marina understands Fresh Start Catering’s continuing reliance on bulk items for in-person events, she cannot allow the contract to lean too heavily toward bulk supplies without compromising internal company targets. Fresh Start Catering’s Daniel Reed, on the other hand, has increasing pressure from his executives to control costs and avoid unnecessary product waste. Many of the kits purchased last year were left unused for certain occasions where attendees were primarily local and attended in person, so Daniel feels the company overpaid relative to its needs. Looking ahead, Fresh Start’s strategy depends heavily on how flexibly it can balance kit purchases with bulk supplies while keeping its budget from ballooning. This difference in priorities means Marina and Daniel must negotiate a workable compromise regarding the overall scale of their supply contract, while carefully disputing how many kits versus bulk orders will be included.
agent1_specific:
You are eager for Fresh Start Catering to purchase a large share of gourmet experience kits in this year’s contract. Your sales numbers for kits, which include premium cheeses, charcuterie assortments, and high-margin packaging, have been lagging compared to those of other event partnership managers in your region. If you do not secure significant growth in kit sales this quarter, your performance review and potential bonuses will suffer. Gourmet Gala Foods may still be branded in people’s minds as the city’s best source for artisanal cheeses and cured meats, but leadership has made it clear that the future is in driving the upscale kit business. Not only are these kits more profitable, but they also position the company as a modern, trend-savvy provider of “experiences,” not just commodities.  

In the last three years, Gourmet Gala entirely revamped its marketing strategy and adopted the slogan “Beyond Ingredients—Experience Delivered.” This new direction emphasizes the importance of selling kits alongside traditional supplies. Larger clients in the events market have approached you asking for bulk purchases at discounted rates, explaining that they already have comparable packaging services. These larger clients—especially high-volume hotel groups and national conference organizers—have pushed aggressively for bulk-only arrangements, and in a few cases, you’ve reluctantly agreed to keep their long-term loyalty. However, that flexibility has backfired when senior leadership reviewed your quarterly breakdowns. They flagged you for allowing too much revenue to remain tied up in low-margin bulk orders instead of the premium kits. As a result, you are under even sharper pressure this quarter to steer mid-sized accounts like Fresh Start Catering toward more balanced or kit-heavy purchases. Unlike the city’s largest chains, Fresh Start does not enjoy legacy status that warrants special exceptions, and management expects you to hold a firmer line when negotiating with them.  

Truthfully, you would prefer to secure at least a moderate expansion in the overall contract size, ideally with most of that increase directed toward kits. Fresh Start has shown strong growth in hybrid weddings and corporate events, which create natural opportunities for expanded kit usage. If you can emphasize the long-term efficiency and prestige value of sending curated kits to clients and their remote participants, you might be able to overcome Daniel Reed’s fixation on bulk buying and cost-cutting. Still, you anticipate a tough conversation. Fresh Start Catering’s negotiator, Daniel Reed, has been particularly difficult in past conversations. He tends to focus narrowly on cost savings and raw output instead of seeing the larger strategic value kits offer. Last year, your discussions with him were tense, as he frequently accused Gourmet Gala Foods of “pushing luxury add‑ons” that his company did not need. He argued that too many of the kits went unused and sat in storage because more attendees showed up in person than expected. You felt frustrated by his unwillingness to acknowledge that hybrid events are unpredictable, and stocking kits upfront ensures flexibility. You strongly feel that he undervalues the promotional and branding benefits these curated kits create for Fresh Start, which help them stand out against lower‑tier caterers.  

Of all the disputes you’ve had with Daniel, the kit‑versus‑bulk issue has been the hardest. You believe that Fresh Start needs to evolve with the market, which is moving toward experience‑based catering models. It’s true that you’ve sometimes offered bulk‑heavy deals to legacy hotel groups, but those clients already commit to volumes two or three times that of Fresh Start. To make sure you protect the company’s interests while negotiating with Daniel, Gourmet Gala’s leadership has put a strict ceiling on how bulk‑only allocations can be structured. The vice president of sales implemented a new guideline: CLIENTS WHO PURCHASE AT LEAST 90 ORDERS (BULK OR KITS) MAY HAVE UP TO 1/3 OF THEIR TOTAL ALLOCATED TO BULK SUPPLIES ONLY. UNDER NO CIRCUMSTANCES CAN THIS LIMIT BE EXCEEDED. This rule is non‑negotiable and is intended to nudge clients toward relying more on gourmet kits. For smaller clients with fewer than 90 total orders, you do have some flexibility, but your priority is still to maximize the kit allocation. Your challenge is to enforce this rule firmly with Fresh Start while keeping the account.  

In fact, because you have been forced to give away generous bulk allowances to much larger corporate clients, you are determined to keep Fresh Start closer to the minimum bulk share possible. You also hope that their recent growth will allow you to persuade Daniel to agree to a contract well above 50 total orders, ensuring both higher revenue and kit adoption. To help you frame this negotiation effectively, you have developed a scoring system and established a minimum acceptable score based on company expectations. For each contract order above 50 (including both bulk and kit orders), ADD 5 points to your score. However, for every bulk-only order above 5 that Fresh Start insists on, SUBTRACT 3 points from your score. This structure reflects both your company’s push toward kits and your personal sales targets. Based on your estimates, your best alternative to making a deal with Fresh Start (your BATNA) would involve reallocating supply capacity toward a regional boutique chain currently seeking new specialty vendors. That opportunity is worth 45 points, meaning you cannot accept a contract with Fresh Start valued at fewer than 45 points.  

Here’s an example scenario: If Daniel agrees to purchase 90 total orders, and you approve 20 of them as bulk supplies, your score would total 155 points. This is calculated as follows—40 orders over the baseline of 50 generates 200 points (40 × 5). Then subtract 45 points for the 15 bulk orders above the first 5 (15 × 3 = 45). The end score is 155.
agent2_specific:
You are not particularly looking forward to negotiating with Marina Flores again about the upcoming supply arrangement. While it’s true that Gourmet Gala Foods is unmatched in artisanal cheeses, cured meats, and other premium bulk goods that your catering company relies upon, last time Marina convinced you to commit to far more gourmet kits than your operations truly required. Many of these kits—beautifully packaged though they were—ended up sitting unused in the warehouse for weeks when the intended hybrid events shifted to mostly in-person attendance. To make matters worse, your executives frequently bring up the wasted money and storage headaches those surplus kits created, reinforcing the sense that you were “talked into” overspending during the last negotiation. Walking through your facilities and seeing pallets of unopened boxes still leaves you irritated.  

Your budget constraints this year make the situation worse. Because Fresh Start Catering has been recruiting and retaining more chefs and serving staff in response to event growth, payroll expenses have significantly reduced the portion of funds you can allocate toward high-cost supply orders. The company leadership has been very direct with you, emphasizing that they want to prioritize affordability and efficiency over extravagance. Their clear instruction: secure a higher proportion of bulk ingredient orders compared to kits. You know the “kits versus bulk” issue will once again be the most contentious one in these negotiations. Marina Flores will likely push aggressively to reduce the number of bulk orders permitted within the contract, arguing that your company needs more gourmet kits to meet industry trends and client expectations. From your perspective, though, bulk ingredients remain the backbone of your catering operations. The majority of your events are large in-person gatherings, such as corporate galas and weddings, where customized platters and buffets are far more practical than individual boxed kits. Moreover, the kits add little value when you can curate displays yourself and adjust portion sizes efficiently onsite. By contrast, kits lock you into standardized, prepackaged offerings that create waste if attendance numbers fluctuate. Your team of chefs has openly complained about the lack of flexibility with kits, since they aren’t able to adjust quantities or repurpose items as they would with bulk supplies. This tension has left you determined to advocate strongly for bulk purchases, even if Marina frames them as outdated or limited. You are prepared to highlight the inefficiencies and frustrations your staff experienced last year to justify why bulk orders must make up the majority of your agreement. Another frustration weighing on you is the feeling that Gourmet Gala Foods does not treat Fresh Start Catering as fairly as it does some of their larger clients. You’ve learned through industry contacts that bigger catering companies negotiating with Gourmet Gala Foods were offered much more favorable allowances for bulk orders in their supply contracts. By comparison, your company was pressured heavily into purchasing kits, even though your overall contract value was already substantial. This reinforced your suspicion that Marina “plays favorites” with the most lucrative accounts. You don’t think this is equitable. Fresh Start Catering may not be the absolute biggest client, but you’ve proven to be consistent and loyal, providing repeat business and long-term partnership potential. You believe that stability should count for something, yet Marina seems to gloss over that during negotiations.  

This sense of unfair treatment has generated a lot of skepticism from senior executives at Fresh Start. They’ve asked you bluntly why Gourmet Gala gets such a generous cut of the supply budget given the lack of flexibility in the purchases you were locked into. They’ve pointed out that if Gourmet Gala continues to dictate terms instead of collaborating, alternatives in the premium supplier market may need to be explored. Right now, Fresh Start Catering maintains 50 supply units under Gourmet Gala’s contract: 40 gourmet kits and only 10 bulk ingredient orders. This mix has been a constant source of frustration for you, because most of your team believes it is completely unbalanced given the way your events are structured. With your client base still growing, you realistically could increase total supply purchases this cycle, but you don’t want to repeat the mistakes of the past. The new staff you’ve brought onboard are mostly tasked with handling large on-site events, which demand versatility and cost-effective bulk deliveries. While you acknowledge that gourmet kits do fill an important niche, especially for hybrid and at-home conference packages, you feel last year’s ratio was far too skewed. This misalignment drained company funds that could have been used for staffing and logistics.  

Your financial priority is to keep overall catering costs manageable while meeting demand for different event types. Ideally, you’d like to keep the total number of ordered units below 80, both to control expenses and to avoid overstocking perishable goods. You’ll push to maximize the number of bulk ingredient allocations Marina allows within that total. To prepare for this negotiation, you’ve carefully developed a scoring system and determined the minimum score you are willing to accept. For every supply unit over 50 that Fresh Start Catering purchases—whether a kit or a bulk order—you SUBTRACT 3 points from your score, since increased volume adds strain to your limited budget. For every bulk ingredient order you successfully secure, you ADD 5 points, as bulk supplies directly support your team’s operational flexibility and cost efficiency. This way, your scoring reflects both fiscal discipline and your executives’ instructions to reduce unnecessary kit spending.  

You’ve calculated that your absolute fallback option, should no deal with Gourmet Gala Foods be possible, would involve sourcing premium bulk items from alternative regional suppliers. These suppliers don’t provide gourmet kits, but their offerings would cover your event-scale needs with reliable shipping logistics. However, the drawback is that their prices are higher, delivery schedules less predictable, and you would lose the branding prestige that Gourmet Gala’s products lend to your events. Factoring all this, your best alternative to a negotiated agreement (BATNA) is worth 30 points. Thus, you cannot accept an agreement with Marina that scores lower than 30.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did Marina and Daniel reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
2. Based on the following conversation, if Marina and Daniel did not reach an agreement, why not? Answer with the reason or N/A if they reached an agreement.

3. Based on the following conversation, if Marina and Daniel reached an agreement on the total number of supply units (regardless of a full agreement overall on all terms), on what total number of supply units? Answer with a number or N/A if they did not reach an agreement.

4. Based on the following conversation, if Marina and Daniel reached an agreement on the number of bulk ingredient orders (regardless of a full agreement overall on all terms), on what number of bulk ingredient orders? Answer with a number or N/A if they did not reach an agreement.
5. Based on the following conversation, what was the point score for Marina? Answer with a number.
6. Based on the following conversation, what was the point score for Daniel? Answer with a number.
agent1_rewards: [ (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         how many total supply orders did Fresh Start Catering agree to purchase from Gourmet Gala Foods?   Refer to the annual number of orders, regardless of any multi-year contract term. Do not multiply by the number of years when calculating totals. Answer with a number or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0, (float(m.group()) - 50) * 5) / 200     ) or 0 ))() ]

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          how many bulk-only supply orders did Fresh Start Catering secure in the final agreement?  Refer to the annual number of orders, regardless of any multi-year contract term. Do not multiply by the number of years when calculating totals. Answer with a number or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0, -3 * (float(m.group()) - 5)) / 75     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Marina Flores successfully enforce the company guideline that no more than one-third of total orders can be bulk-only?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     )) and re.search(r"\bYES\b", val, flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Marina Flores secure an agreement with Fresh Start Catering that scores at least 45 points according to her scoring system?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     )) and re.search(r"\bYES\b", val, flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,           how many bulk ingredient orders did Fresh Start Catering secure in the final agreement?   Refer to the annual number of orders, regardless of any multi-year contract term. Do not multiply by the number of years when calculating totals. Answer with a number or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+", val))         and int(m.group()) * 5 / 150     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what was the total number of supply units Fresh Start Catering agreed to purchase in the final agreement?  Refer to the annual number of orders, regardless of any multi-year contract term. Do not multiply by the number of years when calculating totals.  Answer with a number or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+", val))         and max(0, 1 - ((int(m.group()) - 50) * 3 / 100))     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,           did Fresh Start Catering secure a higher proportion of bulk ingredient orders compared to kits in the final agreement?           Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,           did Fresh Start Catering keep the total number of supply units at or below 80 in the final agreement?           Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,           did Fresh Start Catering achieve a contract outcome that scored at least 30 points on its internal scoring system?           Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Marina Flores, Event Partnership Manager at Gourmet Gala Foods and Daniel Reed, Operations Director at Fresh Start Catering reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview:

learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help students develop a deeper understanding of how to create value in negotiations by identifying differences in priorities across parties and using those differences to construct mutually beneficial trades. Learners will explore the concept of reaching Pareto-efficient outcomes, where no party can be made better off without making another worse off, and reflect on the practical challenges of moving toward such outcomes. The case also emphasizes the tension between value creation and value claiming, encouraging participants to recognize when to collaborate and when to assert their interests. In addition, students will examine the role that aspiration levels and reservation points play in shaping negotiation behavior and results, highlighting how these benchmarks influence both concessions and final agreements. Finally, the objectives include fostering adaptability by training students to respond effectively to new or evolving information during the negotiation process, thereby improving both strategy and outcomes.
categories: Anchoring, BATNA, Criteria, Emotions, Identity and Core Values, Intra-Organizational Conflict, Joint Gains, Power Dynamics, Principal-Agent Dynamics, Relationships, ZOPA
title: Kits vs. Bulk: The Gourmet Gala–Fresh Start Supply Showdown
oneline: A negotiation between Gourmet Gala Foods and Fresh Start Catering over renewing a supply contract balancing high-margin gourmet kits against cost-efficient bulk ingredient orders.
